title: USD feels safer. It costs you 2% more.

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD because it feels safer, you're paying 2% extra for that feeling. Here's the math. Your USD wire to a Chinese factory goes through two exchange rate conversions. Your bank takes 0.5 to 1.5% turning your local currency into USD. Your supplier's bank takes another 0.3 to 0.8% turning USD back into CNY. That stack is invisible because it's baked into the rate, not the fee line. On a 20,000 dollar order, you've quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear. So next quote, they pad your USD price by 2 to 5% to cover the wait. Stop paying twice for the same feeling. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

So next quote, they pad your USD price by 2 to 5% to cover the wait. Worth knowing before the next PI lands. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Picture this. You run a small business. You buy stuff from a factory in China. Every few months you send them money. Maybe twenty thousand dollars at a time. You send it in US dollars because that's what everyone does. It feels safe. It feels normal. But here's what's actually happening behind the scenes. Your bank doesn't just send the dollars. First, your bank turns your home currency into US dollars. They take a tiny cut of the exchange. About half a percent to one and a half percent. You never see this on your bill because it's hidden inside the exchange rate itself. Then the money lands in your supplier's bank in China. But here's the thing. Chinese banks don't really hold US dollars. So the Chinese bank flips it again into Chinese yuan. They take another cut. About a third of a percent to almost one percent. Two banks. Two cuts. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that's anywhere from one sixty to four sixty gone. Just gone. Before the goods even ship. And it gets worse. Your supplier waits seven to ten days for the dollars to clear customs and paperwork in China. That wait costs them cash flow. So next time they quote you, they quietly bump the price two to five percent to cover the wait. You're paying extra twice for the same order. Now at WorldFirst, we do this differently. We pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. Same supplier. Same bank account. Same payment. Half the cost. Worth knowing before your next order. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Cold open + compounding math
scripts.original_vo: Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20,000 dollars in USD because it feels safer. It just cost you 460 dollars extra and you didn't feel a thing. Here's why. Two conversions. Your bank does USD on the way out, 1.5% margin, 300 dollars. Supplier's bank does CNY on the way in, 0.8% margin, 160 dollars. Total leakage: 460 dollars. Now run that across the year. 12 orders. That's 5,500 dollars sitting inside someone else's spread. Run two years. 11,000. Run five. 27,500. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee line. It's the rate. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. 60 to 100 dollars on the same 20K order. The other 360 stays in your business. Every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's the part most business owners miss about paying suppliers in USD. The USD price on the PI is not locked in. You see 20,000 USD and think that's the cost. Wrong. Two conversions happen. Your bank converts your local currency to USD at their card rate, not the rate on Google. That's 0.5 to 1.5% margin you never see. Then the wire lands in your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts USD to CNY at their card rate. Another 0.3 to 0.8% margin. Stacked. Invisible. Baked into the rate, never the fee line. On a 20K order that's 160 to 460 dollars vanished into spread. Plus your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for clearance and SAFE compliance review. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. 80% land same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's the part most business owners miss when paying a Chinese supplier in US dollars. The price on the quote is not the real price. You see twenty thousand dollars on the invoice and you think that's what it costs. It isn't. Let us walk you through what actually happens. When you send the payment, your bank changes your home currency into US dollars first. But not at the rate you see on Google. They use their own rate, which is a bit worse. That gap is half a percent to one and a half percent of your money. You never see it. Then the dollars travel to China and land at your supplier's bank. Here's something most people don't know. Chinese bank accounts mostly hold Chinese yuan, not dollars. So the moment the dollars arrive, the bank has to change them again. They also use their own rate. Another third of a percent to almost one percent. Two changes. Two slices. Both buried inside the exchange rate, not on any fee line. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that's a hundred and sixty to four hundred and sixty dollars just gone. Plus there's a wait. Chinese rules require a compliance check on incoming dollar wires. Your supplier sits on the money for about a week before they can actually use it. At WorldFirst, we built a different pipe. We send Chinese yuan directly into your supplier's account using the local China rails. One exchange. Half a percent or less, total. Eighty percent of payments land the same day. Search WorldFirst.

Your bank's USD conversion margin, 0.5 to 1.5%, hidden in the rate. Two. Your supplier's bank CNY conversion margin, 0.3 to 0.8%, hidden in their rate. Three. Their 7 to 10 day wait for the USD to clear, which costs them cash flow. Four. The 2 to 5% padding they bake into the next USD quote to cover that wait. Add it up on a 20,000 dollar order. 460 dollars in spread. 700 dollars in next-quote padding. 1,160 dollars on one purchase order. And the USD price never even moved on paper. Real talk. The dollar feels safer because it's the currency you think in. It is not the cheapest currency to send to China. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same supplier. Same bank account. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst. Period.

So why are you paying a Shenzhen factory in dollars? Here's what happens. Your bank converts your local currency to USD, 1% margin. Their bank converts USD to CNY, 0.5% margin. Two conversions on one payment. 300 dollars gone on a 20K order. Plus a 7 to 10 day wait for clearance. Plus a padded quote next time. WorldFirst is built for this. Pays the supplier in CNY directly over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. Same bank account they already use. Use the currency the country uses. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody's telling you about paying suppliers in USD. The dollar doesn't protect you. It just hides where the money goes. Your bank quotes USD. You think the price is locked. It isn't. They convert your local currency at their card rate, 1% margin. The USD lands at your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts again, 0.5% margin. Two banks. Two spreads. Both invisible. On a 20K order, 300 dollars vanishes inside the rate. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear, takes the currency risk, and pads the next quote to cover both. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. WorldFirst pays in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. The rate you see is the rate the supplier gets. That's the difference. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's something nobody is telling you about paying your supplier in US dollars. The dollar doesn't really protect you. It just hides where your money actually goes. Let us show you. Your bank gives you a price in US dollars. You see that number and you think, okay, that's the price. Locked in. It is not locked in. The first thing they do is change your home currency into US dollars using their own internal rate. Not the rate you see online. Their rate is a tiny bit worse. About one percent worse. That gap, you never see. Now the dollars travel over to your supplier's bank in China. Here's something most people don't realise. Chinese bank accounts don't really hold US dollars day to day. They hold Chinese yuan. So the moment the dollars land, the bank changes them again. Another half a percent gap. Two banks. Two hidden gaps. Both completely invisible to you. On a twenty thousand dollar order, three hundred dollars just disappears inside the rate. Nobody points to it. Nobody charges you a fee for it. Then your supplier waits seven to ten days for the dollars to clear. During that week, they're carrying the currency risk. They don't know if the yuan rate will shift. So the next time they quote you, they pad the price to cover both the wait and the risk. This is how it works on every single dollar wire to China. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. The rate you see really is the rate your supplier gets. That's the difference. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: For every business owner who likes to quote prices in US dollars because it just feels more professional. We hear you. The dollar feels safer. The dollar feels like the grown up currency. But it costs you about two percent more on every single order to a Chinese supplier. Let us walk you through why. Picture this. You wire twenty thousand dollars to your factory in Shenzhen. Your bank takes about one percent on the way out when they change your home currency into dollars. Then your supplier's bank takes another half a percent on the way in when they change those dollars into Chinese yuan, because that's what they actually use. That's three hundred dollars. Gone. Before the goods even touch a container. After that, your supplier has to wait about a week for the dollars to clear Chinese paperwork called SAFE compliance. While the money sits, they're carrying the currency risk. They don't know if yuan moves against the dollar in that week. To cover that risk and that wait, they quietly pad the next quote they send you. Two to five percent. So you're paying for a safety that exists in your head, not in your books. This happens every week. To real businesses. Every single week. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. Same bank account they already have. Same supplier. One exchange at half a percent or less. Half the cost. And in eighty percent of cases, the money lands the same day. Search WorldFirst.

PH1.analysis: Core mechanism (two stacked FX margins on USD wire to China, supplier wait, padded next quote) is grounded in the card findings. The 0.5 to 1.5% sender-side and 0.3 to 0.8% receiver-side margins are typical-case ranges, defensible. The 'next quote padded 2 to 5%' is a real industry pattern but presented as universal when it's situational. 'Half the cost' is roughly accurate against the 0.8-2.3% USD stack vs 0.3-0.5% CNY route, but supplier-side bank fees on CNY receipt can still apply in edge cases. WorldFirst CNY-direct claim aligns with CIPS/MYbank rails described in findings.

skeptic_comments.text: Where got 2%? My Maybank quote not so off lah.
My supplier always quote in USD only. Never offer CNY price.
Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. Got MAS license?
Sounds like ad
How they get same-day to china? Public holiday how?

PH2.analysis: Compounding math (460 per order x 12 = 5,500/yr) is internally consistent with the per-order figure. Assumes constant order size and frequency, which is a model not a fact. The hire/container/school-fees framing is rhetorical, not load-bearing. The 1.5% sender + 0.8% receiver high-end is real but cherry-picked at the upper bound. WorldFirst CNY-direct cost claim (60-100 on 20K at 0.3-0.5%) aligns with findings.

skeptic_comments.text: 460 sounds inflated. My BCA rate not that bad.
12 orders a year very different from my volume. I do 4.
Container? bro it's a tiktok not a sales call
But what if cny rate moves against me? Usd more predictable for me
Show me a real receipt

PH3.analysis: Accusation framing ('protects your bank, not you') is sharp but defensible because the bank is the structural beneficiary of FX margin. Two-conversion mechanism and the 2-5% padded-next-quote are both in findings. 'You just paid extra twice' is a fair summary. Risk: 'familiar, not cheaper, not faster' is accurate but USD is sometimes faster in practice for SEA banks without strong CNY rails, so 'not faster' is situational.

skeptic_comments.text: Familiar IS protection when supplier disputes. Try arguing in CNY.
ok but invoice in usd is industry standard, can't just switch
this is one of those finance influencer ads right
My accountant only knows usd. CNY books become a mess.
0.3% sounds too good. What's the catch?

PH4.analysis: Most mechanism-tight script in the pack. Card-rate vs Google-rate framing is accurate. SAFE compliance review on inbound USD to Chinese accounts is real. CIPS/MYbank claim with 80% same-day matches findings exactly. 'Half the cost' is defensible. Main minor stretch: '7 to 10 day wait' is the upper-bound supplier-side delay; many wires clear in 3 to 5 days in practice. Not a fact issue, just a typical-case framing presented as norm.

skeptic_comments.text: SAFE review 7-10 days? My supplier never mentioned this delay.

How big is the company? Singapore got branch?
Bookmark, will check later

PH5.analysis: Four-cost stack (sender margin, receiver margin, supplier wait, padded quote) is grounded in findings. Math (460 spread + 700 padding = 1,160 on 20K) is the upper bound, presented confidently. The 700 padding figure assumes 3.5% next-quote padding, which is the midpoint of 2-5% range. Defensible but additive presentation makes it feel guaranteed when 3 and 4 are next-order effects, not same-order. Closing claim 'half the cost' aligns with findings.

PG1.analysis: Most balanced script — uses mid-range numbers (1% + 0.5% = 1.5% stack on 20K = 300 dollars) which is closer to real-world average than upper-bound figures. Two-conversion + padded-quote mechanism is grounded. 'Dollar isn't safer when sending to a country that doesn't use it' is a clean reframe, defensible. WorldFirst CNY-direct claim aligns.

skeptic_comments.text: 300 on 20K — sounds about right actually

PG2.analysis: Compound math (300 x 12 = 3,600/yr, 18K over 5 years) is mathematically clean. Underlying assumption that volume and order size stay constant for 5 years is a model. 'USD price feels locked in but it isn't' references the PI-vs-settlement gap from findings. 'Most expensive habit' is rhetorical, defensible at scale.

PG3.analysis: Vietnam-baht analogy is a strong rhetorical move but technically imperfect because Vietnam factories can and sometimes do accept USD as a stable peg currency, so the parallel isn't perfectly clean. Mechanism numbers (1% + 0.5%, 300 on 20K) are mid-range and defensible. CIPS/MYbank claim and same-bank-account-they-already-use are in findings. 'Wrong tool' framing is sharp.

PG4.analysis: Cleanest mechanism explanation in the GV pack. Card-rate, two conversions, supplier currency risk, padded next quote — all in findings. 'Rate you see is the rate the supplier gets' is roughly accurate for CNY-direct (still has 0.3-0.5% margin, but it's transparent). Risk: 'doesn't protect you, just hides where the money goes' is opinion presented as truth, fine for the GV voice.

PG5.analysis: 'Safety that exists in your head, not in your books' is the sharpest reframe in the pack. Mechanism numbers (1% + 0.5%, 300 on 20K) are mid-range. SAFE compliance and currency-risk-while-it-sits are in findings. 'Same-day in 80% of cases' is verbatim from the card findings. Main risk: 'every week, every single week' is anecdotal escalation, not a fact, but it reads as voice rather than data so the red flag stays low-mid.
